title: Parkin
subtitle: Dense Yorkshire oatmeal cake with treacle and ginger
excerpt: A sticky, dark Yorkshire bake made with oatmeal, golden syrup, treacle, and ground ginger. It comes out of the oven firm and almost dry, then softens over two or three days in a tin into something much denser and stickier. Cut into 16 squares.
sourceNotes: Yorkshire parkin is documented from at least the eighteenth century, traditionally associated with Bonfire Night celebrations in northern England. Recipes appear in pre-1928 Yorkshire household cookery manuscripts and in early-twentieth-century northern English baking columns in the British Newspaper Archive. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), gives a comparable treacle and oatmeal bake under regional variations. The characteristic resting period after baking is noted in all historical accounts.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Parkin must rest for at least 2 days in an airtight tin before cutting. Three days is better. Do not cut it before it has rested.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 170°C. Parkin bakes low and slow. The top will look set while the centre is still soft; trust the skewer test.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly baked parkin is dry, crumbly, and disappointing. Left sealed in a tin for 2 to 3 days, it undergoes a transformation: the oatmeal absorbs moisture from the treacle and syrup, the crumb tightens and becomes dense, and the surface turns sticky. This resting period is not optional; it is the recipe. Cutting parkin early is the single most common mistake.

content.text: Parkin is a northern English bake traditionally made around Bonfire Night in autumn. It comes out of the oven looking like a mediocre gingerbread. After two or three days sealed in a tin, it transforms: the oatmeal and treacle draw together into something dense, dark, and fudgy. This
is not a suggestion. Sixteen squares.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan (170°C conventional). Grease and line the tin with baking paper. Melt the butter, golden syrup, treacle, and sugar together in a medium saucepan over low heat, stirring until combined. Remove from the heat and allow to cool for 5 minutes.

Combine the flour, oats, ginger, and bicarbonate of soda in a large bowl. Stir in the cooled syrup mixture, then add the milk and egg and mix until a thick batter forms. Pour into the prepared tin. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes, until set in the centre when pressed and a skewer comes out clean; the surface will remain dark and sticky. Leave to cool completely in the tin, then wrap the whole slab tightly in foil or seal in an airtight tin. Do not cut for at least 2 days.
